title: Move DIV/REM off the fast ALU path
motivation:
The current EX-stage ALU carries fully combinational divide and remainder
hardware even though CoreMark spends very little time in DIV/REM. That rare
logic still taxes timing closure and routing on the Tang Nano 20K, and the
bench core is already close to its LUT budget.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current EX-stage ALU carries fully combinational divide and remainder
hardware even though CoreMark spends very little time in DIV/REM. That rare
logic still taxes timing closure and routing on the Tang Nano 20K, and the
bench core is already close to its LUT budget.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the single-cycle DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU datapath with a small iterative
divider/remainder micro-unit, while keeping the common integer ALU and MUL
path single-cycle. EX would launch the divider on DIV/REM ops, freeze the
front of the pipeline until completion, and then retire normally with the
same RV32M corner-case semantics. This should remove a rarely useful critical
path from normal cycles, improve Fmax and placement slack, and cost little
CoreMark/MHz because CoreMark is dominated by adds, loads, stores, branches,
and plain MUL rather than division. Using an 8-cycle worst-case divider keeps
the added stall bounded enough to stay compatible with the existing formal
depth budget.

changes.description: Add a compact iterative signed/unsigned divider-remainder unit with RV32M divide-by-zero and overflow behavior.
Remove the combinational DIV/REM datapath from the fast ALU path and keep common arithmetic, shifts, compares, and MUL results single-cycle.
Detect DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU, launch the divider, hold EX state while it runs, and only write EX/MEM when a result is ready.
Add an EX structural-stall input so IF/ID freeze cleanly during multicycle divide without breaking redirect or load-use handling.
Wire the divider busy/done handshake between EX and the global stall network while preserving the existing top-level IO and RVFI contract.
Add regression coverage for multicycle divide stalls, forwarding recovery after divider completion, and preserved retirement ordering.

title: Add a small redirect-aware fetch queue
motivation:
The current front-end is tightly coupled to `io_imemReady`: any cycle with
instruction-memory backpressure immediately becomes a decode bubble because
IF emits a NOP and the PC stops. That is especially expensive here because
the fitness run injects random iStalls by design, so front-end elasticity is
worth more than a tiny local logic cleanup.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current front-end is tightly coupled to `io_imemReady`: any cycle with
instruction-memory backpressure immediately becomes a decode bubble because
IF emits a NOP and the PC stops. That is especially expensive here because
the fitness run injects random iStalls by design, so front-end elasticity is
worth more than a tiny local logic cleanup.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the single-slot combinational fetch path with a small speculative
fetch queue in `if_stage`, likely 2-4 entries deep, that buffers `{pc,
instr}` pairs when `io_imemReady` is high and feeds ID from the queue when
the backend is ready. On redirects, flush younger queued instructions and
restart fetch at the resolved target; on load-use or dmem stalls, preserve
the buffered instructions instead of re-fetching them. This is a structural
front-end change, not a predictor: it should raise CoreMark iterations/cycle
under the orchestrator's random stall model with only modest area/timing
cost and without changing the single-retire RVFI contract.

Wire any new fetch-queue status/control signals between IF and hazard logic while keeping the top-level bus and RVFI ports unchanged.
Add cocotb coverage for intermittent `io_imemReady` stalls and redirect flushes while buffered instructions are in flight.

title: Static IF branch and JAL predictor
motivation:
`cores/bench` still fetches strictly `pc+4` until EX resolves every branch
and jump, so each taken loop backedge and direct call burns wrong-path
bubbles. The bench core is already close to its LUT budget, so the best
predictor bet is a zero-table frontend change that improves CoreMark control
flow without adding BTB, RAS, or counter-RAM state.

LUT-tight bench core.

title: Precise cold DIV/REM interlock
motivation:
The current bench ALU keeps DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU in the same combinational
EX-stage result path as ADD, address generation, branch resolution, and MUL.
That divider logic is cold in CoreMark's timed region but still taxes Fmax on
the Tang Nano 20K. The prior broken attempt showed the performance idea is
still attractive, but it must be implemented as a precise long-op interlock
that preserves the full EX instruction context until completion.

hypothesis.motivation:

EX-stage result path as ADD, address generation, branch resolution, and MUL.
That divider logic is cold in CoreMark's timed region but still taxes Fmax on

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move only DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU into a small iterative `div_unit` and leave the
common RV32I operations plus MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU on the existing one-cycle
path. When a divide-class instruction reaches EX, snapshot the full decoded
instruction context and the forwarded operands into shadow registers, assert
an `ex_long_busy` interlock, bubble EX/MEM while the divider runs, and emit
exactly one EX/MEM result when the unit completes. This removes the cold
divider from the universal ALU path, should improve Fmax with negligible
CoreMark IPC loss, and directly addresses the earlier failure mode of stale
EX/MEM retirement state leaking through during a multi-cycle operation.

title: Operand-Stage-Aware Load Interlock
motivation:
The current hazard unit stalls on any raw `rs1`/`rs2` bitfield match behind a
load, even when the younger instruction does not actually read that field yet
or only needs it later as store data in MEM. That conservative policy burns
avoidable one-cycle bubbles in CoreMark's dense load/address/store code, and
the recent `bench` regressions already showed that extra front-end machinery
is a worse trade here than tightening the backend interlock.

hypothesis.motivation:

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Rework the load-use interlock around the stage where an operand is actually
consumed. Add decode metadata for real `rs1`/`rs2` usage, treat STORE `rs2`
as a late MEM-stage consumer instead of an EX-stage consumer, and add a
WB-to-MEM store-data bypass so `load -> store` pairs with an independent
address can proceed without the textbook bubble. This should recover
CoreMark IPC from both false-positive load stalls and true-but-late
store-data dependencies, while keeping load data off the EX ALU/branch path
and avoiding another fetch-path timing penalty.

title: Redirect-aware replay target predictor
motivation:
The current `bench` frontend already proved that a tiny registered replay
predictor can recover a meaningful slice of random `io_imemReady` stalls
without hurting the IF critical path. Its remaining blind spot is taken
control flow: every EX redirect clears the replay candidate, so hot loop
backedges and direct-call targets lose replay coverage exactly where
CoreMark revisits the same PCs most often.

title: DSP-friendly corrected shared multiplier
motivation:
The current best `bench` RTL already recovered the bigger IPC wins, so the
next micro-opt is most likely another Fmax-and-LUT cleanup in the hot ALU
datapath. `rtl/alu.sv` now shares `MUL*` through one 33x33 signed product,
which was a good first step, but it still widens both operands by a sign bit
and routes a 66-bit product even for plain `MUL` and `MULHU`. On the Tang
Nano 20K, trimming that extra width and glue logic is attractive because the
core is still above its LUT target and the previous multiplier cleanup already
proved this region is fitness-sensitive.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the current sign-extended 33x33 shared multiply with a DSP-friendlier
32x32 unsigned product plus small signed-high correction terms for `MULH` and
`MULHSU`. `MUL` can still take the low 32 bits directly, `MULHU` can use the
raw high half, and the signed variants can derive their high halves by
subtracting the operand-dependent correction terms instead of carrying an
extra sign bit through the full multiplier. This keeps all `MUL*` operations
single-cycle, avoids the control risk of a multicycle M-unit, preserves the
existing DIV/REM and ALTOPS behavior, and should further reduce multiplier
routing pressure and LUT glue without giving back the load-bypass or replay
IPC gains.

title: One-entry posted store buffer
motivation:
The current pipeline freezes the whole machine on any store when `io_dmemReady`
drops, even though a younger run of ALU, branch, or jump instructions could
execute correctly once the store address, write data, and byte mask are already
known. Prior structural attempts showed that adding more front-end or ID-stage
control logic is expensive on this core, so the best remaining structural gain
is backend decoupling that leaves IF/ID timing alone.

hypothesis.motivation:

control logic is expensive on this core, so the best remaining structural gain

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a single-entry posted-store buffer that captures a store's aligned address,
replicated write data, byte mask, and RVFI-visible metadata when the store
reaches MEM. If the external dmem port stalls, retire the store from the buffer
immediately, keep draining the buffered write in the background, and allow
younger non-memory instructions to keep flowing. Only stall when a younger
load or store reaches MEM while the buffer is still occupied, preserving
in-order memory semantics without reintroducing load-use or control-path
complexity. This should recover CoreMark IPC from random dmem backpressure on
store-heavy regions while avoiding the IF/ID critical-path regressions seen in
earlier structural experiments.

title: Conflict-tolerant replay predictor
motivation:
The current best `bench` frontend already gets real value from the tiny
sequential replay predictor, which means the safest remaining predictor win
is probably higher replay hit rate rather than deeper control speculation.
Today the table is only 8 entries and direct-mapped, so hot CoreMark PCs
that alias on the same low index bits can evict each other and turn later
random `io_imemReady=0` cycles back into bubbles.

title: EX fast-path add/address bypass
motivation:
The largest safe `bench` gains have come from local backend changes that
recovered IPC or reduced hot-path routing without widening the front-end
control logic. After the replay cache, MEM-to-EX load bypass, and posted
store buffer, the next likely win is Fmax: the common EX add/address path
still goes through the same mixed-function ALU block that also carries
shifts, compares, the shared multiplier, and combinational DIV/REM logic.

title: Alias-aware load bypass around posted stores
motivation:
The current best `bench` core already gets a real win from the one-entry
posted store buffer, but it still serializes every younger load behind any
buffered main-memory store. CoreMark mixes loads and stores densely, and many
of those younger loads are independent of the older store, so the blanket
stall likely leaves IPC on the table even after the successful store-buffer
change.

hypothesis.motivation:

buffered main-memory store. CoreMark mixes loads and stores densely, and many

stall likely leaves IPC on the table even after the successful store-buffer
change.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing posted store buffer, but make it address-aware instead of
blocking all younger memory ops. When a younger load reaches MEM while a
main-memory store is buffered, compare the load's aligned word address and
byte mask against the buffered store: if there is no overlap, let the load use
the dmem port immediately and drain the buffered store later; if the load is
fully covered by the buffered bytes, forward from the buffer; only stall on
true alias or partial-overlap cases, and keep younger stores conservative.
This is a backend-only structural change that compounds with the winning
posted-store buffer while avoiding the IF/ID timing regressions seen in prior
structural attempts.

title: Dual-candidate control-aware replay predictor
motivation:
The winning predictor on `bench` is the tiny IF replay table, not earlier
branch resolution or heavier control speculation. Recent failures showed that
adding decode-to-PC steering, prediction metadata, or wider replay RAM hurts
either Fmax or formal cost, yet the current single replay candidate still
goes blind across hot taken backedges and direct-call targets because it only
tracks the sequential `pc+4` path.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing replay-table frontend and EX redirect contract, but add a
second replay candidate slot dedicated to likely control successors. When a
live fetch sees a direct JAL or an obvious backward B-type loop branch,
`if_stage` does a decode-light target calculation off the fetched instruction,
looks that target up in the existing `{pc,instr}` replay table, and banks it
as a control-target candidate while the normal sequential `pc+4` candidate

new predicted-PC state in IF/ID/EX; the win comes from having the first
post-redirect target instruction already replayable if `io_imemReady` drops.

stays off the live fetch critical path and avoids the metadata/formal blow-up
that hurt the earlier static and learned next-PC attempts.

motivation:

cone around live `a`/`b`. On Tang Nano 20K, that extra fanout and muxing is a
plausible remaining Fmax tax after the add/address fast path already landed.

title: Two-entry posted store FIFO

than one buffered write, collapses back to a full-pipeline block even though
the pending work is still an in-order sequence of already-resolved stores.

title: Victim-slot replay predictor
motivation:
The strongest predictor result on `bench` is still the tiny sequential replay

control bookkeeping either regressed badly or only recovered a very small
benefit. The remaining plausible predictor win is therefore not deeper branch
speculation, but a cheaper way to recover replay-table conflict misses than
the prior 2-way and control-target variants.

title: Resolve direct JAL in ID
motivation:
The current `bench` core has already captured the best backend decoupling and
replay-table wins, but every unconditional direct call/jump still waits until
EX to redirect fetch. Prior structural history says full ID-stage
branch/JAL/JALR resolution is too expensive on this FPGA, so the next control
improvement should target only the cheapest unconditional case.

motivation:
The existing `bench` predictor win came from the tiny sequential replay path,
while heavier control speculation, wider replay tables, and IF-side target
decode all regressed. The remaining frontend blind spot is the first fetch
after a taken redirect: hot loop headers and helper-call targets are revisited
often, but the current replay candidate is cleared exactly when those PCs
become useful again.

title: Decode-gated operand bypass cleanup
motivation:
The recent writeback-retime regression showed that the remaining backend
limiter is not just where `wb_data` is selected, but the always-on
WB-to-ID read-bypass compares and muxes that sit on both regfile read
ports every cycle. On this core, many hot CoreMark instructions
(`OP-IMM`, `LOAD`, `JALR`, `AUIPC`, `LUI`, `JAL`) do not consume `rs2`,
and some consume neither source, yet the current datapath still drives raw
instruction bitfields through regfile read, bypass, and forwarding logic.
Trimming that unnecessary operand work is a plausible remaining Fmax win
that stays off the fragile IF/control paths.

title: Drain-and-replace posted store slot
motivation:
The one-entry posted store buffer was the strongest structural win on `bench`,
but it still behaves like a zero-depth queue at the handoff point: when a
buffered store drains, any younger store waiting in EX/MEM burns an extra
cycle before it can post. The full two-entry FIFO already failed placement
and alias-aware load bypass hurt timing, so the next backend improvement
needs to preserve the current one-slot footprint while removing that
needless store-to-store bubble.

title: Replay-backed one-slot IF decoupler
motivation:
The current frontend already has a winning sequential replay mechanism, but it
only uses cached instructions as a fallback when `io_imemReady=0`. On a replay
hit with `io_imemReady=1`, the live memory port still spends the cycle
refetching the current instruction instead of getting one step ahead. Prior
structural history says a general fetch FIFO is too expensive on this FPGA, so
the best remaining front-end bet is a much narrower elasticity change that
reuses the existing replay state.

refreshes the lookahead slot/table in the same cycle. Keep the current
redirect/flush rules, keep all state local to `if_stage`, and do not add IF-side

bandwidth, recovering more random iStall loss than the fallback-only replay
path without the timing cost that sank broader fetch-queue and control-predictor
attempts.

a hot path touched by decode reads, write-first bypass, and EX dependencies,
and they likely block the synthesis patterns that would infer cheaper
distributed RAM.

title: Mem-stall replay prefetch slot
motivation:
The current winning frontend only learns future instructions from cycles where
decode itself is advancing. When MEM-side backpressure freezes the pipe, the
instruction port usually sits idle even though those cycles could have been
used to seed the next sequential replay hit. Earlier broad fetch queues lost
because they had to run ahead every cycle; the remaining structural opening is
a narrower overlap of dmem-stall time with harmless prefetch work.

motivation:
The structural history on `bench` says the profitable direction is backend
decoupling, not wider front-end control: the posted store buffer helped, while
broader fetch and decode-time redirect schemes mostly regressed. The biggest
remaining unhidden stall class is a dmem-backed load, which still freezes the
whole machine on any `!io_dmemReady` cycle even though the tournament model's
random backpressure creates many short stalls that could overlap useful younger
non-memory work.

RVFI ordering and in-order memory semantics, avoids putting more logic on the
IF/ID critical path, and targets the same short random dStall bursts that made
the posted store buffer pay off.

title: Backend-stall shadow replay prefetcher
motivation:
The only predictor family that has consistently helped `bench` is the tiny
sequential replay path in `rtl/if_stage.sv`. The failures around branch
prediction, redirect-target seeding, wider replay lookup, and current-PC
fallback all point to the same rule: do not put more logic on the live IF
critical path. The best remaining predictor opportunity is therefore to warm
the existing replay state using cycles that are already lost to backend stalls,
instead of adding new control speculation.

hypothesis.motivation:
The only predictor family that has consistently helped `bench` is the tiny

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the current `fetch_available = imem_ready || replay_hit` contract, the
one-cycle `pc+4` replay candidate, and all EX/ID redirect behavior unchanged,
but add a shadow prefetch mode that runs only while the architectural frontend
is frozen by a backend MEM stall. In that mode, `if_stage` should leave the
visible `pc` and IF->ID payload untouched, drive a non-architectural shadow
fetch for the held `pc+4`, and train the existing replay table/candidate only
if that shadow fetch returns. This targets the current replay predictor's main
blind spot: it only learns future instructions on cycles where decode itself
advances. By harvesting otherwise idle MEM-stall cycles, the core should raise
replay-hit coverage without adding IF-side branch decode, target RAM, second
lookup ways, or cross-stage prediction metadata. Compared with the earlier
broken stall-prefetch attempt, the key constraint is to keep shadow-prefetch
state strictly non-architectural so cosim-visible fetch/retire behavior stays
identical aside from fewer later iStall bubbles.

title: Predecode hot-path JAL and fast-add selects
motivation:
The current best `bench` core already got its biggest IPC lift by resolving
direct `JAL` in ID and another smaller timing win by splitting common
ADD/address work onto a dedicated EX carry-chain path. Those wins now leave
extra control decode on two timing-sensitive cones: the IF->ID->PC feedback
path for early `JAL`, and the EX result-select path that still rebuilds the
add-fast-path condition from several control bits every cycle.

hypothesis.motivation:

path for early `JAL`, and the EX result-select path that still rebuilds the

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing profitable behavior, but predecode the hot operation
classes once in ID and carry the narrow results to where they are consumed.
In `rtl/id_stage.sv`, compute the early direct-`JAL` redirect from a tiny
opcode-plus-J-immediate fast path instead of waiting on the full
decoder/`imm_gen` cone, while still letting misaligned `JAL` fall back to
the existing EX-stage trap path. In parallel, add a latched one-bit
add/address fast-path classification to the ID/EX control bundle so
`rtl/ex_stage.sv` can select the dedicated carry-chain result without
recomputing the current multi-term `use_fast_add` condition. This stays in
the same single-issue 5-stage architecture, preserves the current RVFI and
control-flow behavior, and should recover Fmax by trimming the control cost
of two changes that already proved their IPC value.

title: Word-only load escape around posted stores
motivation:
The best structural win on `bench` so far came from the one-entry posted
store buffer, which confirms that backend decoupling is the right direction
on this FPGA. The next obvious loss is that the current core still blocks
every younger load behind a buffered RAM store, even though CoreMark's code
is heavily dominated by aligned `lw`/`sw` traffic and the earlier losing
alias-aware attempt paid mostly for being fully general across byte masks,
partial overlaps, and load-vs-drain arbitration.

hypothesis.motivation:
The best structural win on `bench` so far came from the one-entry posted
store buffer, which confirms that backend decoupling is the right direction
on this FPGA. The next obvious loss is that the current core still blocks
every younger load behind a buffered RAM store, even though CoreMark's code
is heavily dominated by aligned `lw`/`sw` traffic and the earlier losing

partial overlaps, and load-vs-drain arbitration.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep the existing posted-store buffer, but add a much narrower word-only
escape hatch instead of the earlier fully general alias-aware scheme. When
the buffered store is a normal-RAM full-word `sw`, let a younger aligned
`lw` proceed in one of two cheap cases: if it targets the same aligned word,
forward the buffered store data directly; if it targets a different aligned
word, let the load borrow the dmem port for that cycle and defer the buffered
store drain. Keep byte/halfword accesses, partial-word stores, and all
younger stores on the current conservative stall path. This aims at the
dominant CoreMark memory shape while avoiding the byte-mask overlap logic,
partial forwarding cases, and same-cycle refill feedback that made the prior
broader memory-side structural attempts lose or fail placement.

Relax the store-buffer conflict rule only for the new aligned word-load cases, while preserving full stalls for younger stores, byte/halfword loads, and any ambiguous buffered-store interaction.

Add pipeline tests for buffered `sw` plus same-word `lw` forwarding, different-word `lw` bypass, and conservative stalling on byte/halfword or partial-overlap cases.

title: Consume replay hits as prefetch bandwidth
motivation:
The only predictor family that has clearly helped `cores/bench` is the
existing sequential `{pc,instr}` replay path in `rtl/if_stage.sv`. Right now
that predictor only substitutes for an `io_imemReady=0` bubble. When the
current PC is already replayable and `io_imemReady=1`, the frontend still
spends the live fetch on the same instruction instead of using that cycle to
get one step ahead, leaving random iStall recovery on the table.

hypothesis.motivation:
The only predictor family that has clearly helped `cores/bench` is the
existing sequential `{pc,instr}` replay path in `rtl/if_stage.sv`. Right now
that predictor only substitutes for an `io_imemReady=0` bubble. When the
current PC is already replayable and `io_imemReady=1`, the frontend still
spends the live fetch on the same instruction instead of using that cycle to
get one step ahead, leaving random iStall recovery on the table.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Upgrade the current replay candidate from a stall-only fallback into a
consume-and-prefetch predictor. On a replay hit for the current PC, let decode
consume the cached instruction even if `io_imemReady=1`, and use the live
instruction port in that same cycle to fetch the next sequential PC and bank
it as the next replay candidate. Keep the change entirely inside `if_stage`,
preserve `fetch_available = imem_ready || replay_hit`, and suppress the
prefetch path on redirect/flush cycles so wrong-path lines never become
architecturally visible. This stays in the one predictor design space that
already won on `bench`, but converts replay hits into real one-cycle lookahead
instead of merely avoiding bubbles after a stall.

changes.description: Turn the registered replay slot into a consume-and-prefetch path: on clean replay hits, feed ID from the cached current instruction, drive the live fetch to the next sequential PC, and bank that returned instruction as the next replay candidate without changing redirect or flush semantics.

Add pipeline tests that force replay-hit plus `imem_ready` cycles, verify the prefetch path survives later iStalls, and confirm prefetched lines are discarded correctly across ID-JAL and EX redirect kills.
